data.title: Pfizer, Arvinas breast cancer therapy approved by the FDA
data.description:
The U.S. Food and Drug Administration on Friday approved vepdegestrant, a breast cancer therapy developed by Pfizer (PFE) and Arvinas (ARVN) under the brand name Veppanu. 

Accordingly, the once-daily oral therapy will be available in the U.S. as a late-line option for adults with estrogen receptor-positive, human epidermal growth factor receptor 2-negative, ESR1-mutated advanced or metastatic breast cancer.

Following a trading halt, shares of New Haven, Connecticut-based Arvinas (ARVN) added ~9% in reaction to the decision, which marks its first FDA-approved medicine.

In conjunction with the approval, the FDA also authorized the Guardant360 CDx, a companion diagnostic device developed by Guardant Health (GH) to identify breast cancer harboring ESR1 mutations.

In August, the FDA accepted a New Drug Application submitted by Arvinas (ARVN) for the protein degrader therapy, assigning June 5, 2026, as the target action date.

The NDA was backed by data from the companies’ VERITAC-2 Phase 3 clinical trial, which indicated that patients on vepdegestran survived five months without cancer progression compared to 2.1 months for those who received fulvestrant, an intramuscularly delivered breast cancer drug.

In a statement announcing the approval, Arvinas (ARVN) and Pfizer (PFE) said that they are on track to select a third-party partner that can help realize the full commercial potential of Veppanu.

“Together with Pfizer, we are on track to announce selection of a third party to bring this new treatment option to patients as soon as possible,” Arvinas (ARVN) CEO Randy Teel remarked.

Update: 1:21 pm ET: Adds latest stock reaction and comments from the companies.

data.title: Exxon outlines Golden Pass train 2 mechanical completion by end of 2026 as it targets 1.8 million Permian boe/d in 2026
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Exxon Mobil (XOM) Q1 2026

Management view

  • “We delivered strong operational performance in a challenging environment,” and “excluding identified items and estimated timing effects, our first quarter earnings per share were up versus the fourth quarter of 2025,” according to (Chairman of the Board, President & CEO Darren Woods), who framed results around Middle East disruption, portfolio resilience, and operational response.

  • (Chairman of the Board, President & CEO Darren Woods) described key operating milestones: “We delivered on our plans to increase Permian production year-over-year, achieved record levels of production in Guyana, achieved first LNG at Golden Pass,” and added that “in March, refinery throughput increased by approximately 200,000 barrels a day versus February.”

  • (Chairman of the Board, President & CEO Darren Woods) highlighted LNG and project pipeline positioning: “Train 1 of the facility achieved first LNG in March,” “by the time the third train is online, we will increase the country's current LNG exports by roughly 15%,” and “we continue to progress towards final investment decisions on LNG projects in Papua New Guinea and Mozambique, both expected later this year.”

  • (Senior VP & CFO Neil Hansen) emphasized upstream performance excluding disruptions: “If we take those impacts out, year-over-year, our upstream production was up 8%,” attributing it to “advantaged assets in the Permian and in Guyana.”

Outlook

  • (Chairman of the Board, President & CEO Darren Woods) linked near-term market conditions to the Strait closure and inventory dynamics: “we anticipate as that happens and the Strait remains closed, that we will continue to see increased prices in the marketplace,” and said that after reopening “there's going to be a 1- to 2-month time lag between the Strait opening up and the market seeing normal flow.”

  • (Chairman of the Board, President & CEO Darren Woods) maintained Permian growth framing: “We remain on track to grow full year Permian production to 1.8 million oil equivalent barrels in 2026, with that growth grounded in value, not volume.”

  • (Chairman of the Board, President & CEO Darren Woods) provided Golden Pass timing: “Train 2, we expect to be mechanically complete by the end of this year, and then Train 3 should be mechanically complete as we head into the second quarter of next year.”

  • No revenue or EPS guidance was provided in the prepared remarks or Q&A, so no transcript-supported comparison to analyst estimates was included.

Financial results

  • (Senior VP & CFO Neil Hansen) quantified downstream earnings: “if you take those things aside and you look at that Energy Products segment, we made $2.8 billion in the quarter,” and said it was “up $2 billion compared to last year and a few hundred million compared to the fourth quarter.”

  • (Chairman of the Board, President & CEO Darren Woods) characterized derivative “timing effects” as accounting-driven: “this is truly timing,” with (Senior VP & CFO Hansen) adding it is “driven by the accounting standards and now we have to value these transactions.”

  • No companywide quarterly revenue, net income, or EPS figures were explicitly stated in the transcript.

Q&A

  • Devin McDermott, Morgan Stanley: Asked about timing to normalize operations after the Strait reopens and whether impacts change mid-cycle pricing; (Chairman of the Board, President & CEO Woods) said the market “hasn't seen the full impact…yet,” projected “a 1- to 2-month time lag” post-reopening, and said damaged QatarEnergy trains are “about 3% of our global production,” with repair “anywhere between 3 and 5 years.”

  • Bob Brackett, Bernstein: Asked about March refining margin opportunity and downstream outlook; (Chairman of the Board, President & CEO Woods) said Gulf Coast refineries “ran in the first quarter at record utilization rates” and reiterated “from February to March, we increased refining production by 200,000 barrels a day,” while (Senior VP & CFO Hansen) cited Energy Products earnings of $2.8B.

  • Arun Jayaram, JPMorgan: Asked about Guyana resource expansion and Venezuela; (Chairman of the Board, President & CEO Woods) said Venezuela is “very heavy” and Exxon’s heavy-oil work “positions us uniquely,” while adding Guyana has “record production” and “Uaru expecting first oil late this year.”

  • Neil Mehta, Goldman Sachs: Asked if higher prices change Permian strategy and crude export ban risk; (Chairman of the Board, President & CEO Woods) said “we've had the pedal to the metal,” and warned, “If you shut in exports, you shut in production.”

  • Wei Jiang, Barclays: Asked about LNG macro shifts and ability to accelerate Golden Pass; (Chairman of the Board, President & CEO Woods) said the predicted LNG “length…has gone away,” but “I don't see needle-moving opportunities,” and reiterated Train 2 and Train 3 mechanical completion timing.

  • Doug Leggate, Wolfe Research: Asked about Qatar repair economics versus contract length and force majeure; (Chairman of the Board, President & CEO Woods) said Exxon is “extremely committed” but will do it “in a construct that ensures that we generate a return.”

  • Biraj Borkhataria, RBC: Asked if LNG concentration in Qatar drives faster diversification; (Chairman of the Board, President & CEO Woods) said disruption “doesn't change the opportunity set,” pointing to “Mozambique and Papua New Guinea and continuing to bring on the rest of Golden Pass.”

  • Jason Gabelman, TD Cowen: Asked about insurance and UAE capacity; (Chairman of the Board, President & CEO Woods) said, “I won't get into the specifics of our insurance,” and on the UAE: “we're actively working to help the UAE grow.”

  • Manav Gupta, UBS: Asked about restarting Canada growth and Aspen; (Chairman of the Board, President & CEO Woods) emphasized improving Kearl and Cold Lake competitiveness, calling Kearl “a very competitive source of supply.”

  • Alastair Syme, Citi: Asked about chemical margins and Product Solutions utilization/chemical feedstock; (Chairman of the Board, President & CEO Woods) said Slide 5 shows “industry margins,” and expected elevated crude to provide Exxon a U.S. feed advantage: “we have a big feed advantage there.”

  • Jean Ann Salisbury, BofA: Asked what drives the 3–5 year Qatar repair range; (Chairman of the Board, President & CEO Woods) said cold boxes are “a critical path,” but “I haven't accepted kind of any schedule.”

  • Sam Margolin, Wells Fargo: Asked about reversing timing effects and operating learnings; (Chairman of the Board, President & CEO Woods) said trading “locking in profit” and hedging created “a disconnect,” while (Senior VP & CFO Hansen) said in rising prices “these timing effects typically are negative.”

  • Nitin Kumar, Mizuho: Asked for an update on Gulf Coast power/data center opportunities; (Chairman of the Board, President & CEO Woods) said, “We're not interested in the utility business,” and is focused on “power with virtually…emissions-free” via gas plus CCS, while noting commercial adoption challenges when customers must “pay for the emissions reductions.”

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to pressing on geopolitical disruption, earnings timing effects, and portfolio concentration, including questions on “how lasting you expect the market impacts to be” (Devin McDermott, Morgan Stanley) and whether Qatar repairs are constrained by “limited remaining contract length” (Doug Leggate, Wolfe).

  • Management tone was confident in capability and portfolio resilience, including “We are confident in our advantages” (Chairman of the Board, President & CEO Woods), while also cautious on uncertainty: “difficult to predict exactly how it plays itself out” (Chairman of the Board, President & CEO Woods).

  • Versus the prior quarter’s more transformation-focused messaging, this quarter’s tone centered more on disruption response and logistics, with repeated emphasis on execution under volatility.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • The current call shifted from Q4’s emphasis on multi-year transformation, cost savings, and portfolio strategy to Q1’s emphasis on operational continuity during Middle East disruption, including restoring assets and rerouting supply.

  • Management continuity changed across calls: Q4 included outgoing CFO Kathryn Mikells and CFO succession remarks, while Q1 featured (Senior VP & CFO Neil Hansen) as CFO in role and focused on operational/segment performance and timing effects.

  • Analyst focus shifted from longer-cycle portfolio expansion topics (Q4: Guyana license/force majeure area, upstream portfolio “what’s not in the plan”) toward near-term disruption impacts, Qatar repair duration, downstream margin capture, and accounting timing effects in Q1.

Risks and concerns

  • (Chairman of the Board, President & CEO Woods) warned that if the Strait remains closed, inventories draw down and “we will continue to see increased prices in the marketplace,” while normalization after reopening could take “1- to 2-month” to restore flows.

  • Qatar LNG damage was framed as a multi-year headwind: two trains “were damaged,” representing “about 3% of our global production,” with stated repair time “between 3 and 5 years” (Chairman of the Board, President & CEO Woods).

  • Additional disruption exposures cited included “impacts in Kazakhstan from drone attacks” and a “significant impact from the winter storm in the Permian” (Senior VP & CFO Hansen).

Final takeaway

Management presented Q1 2026 as a test of ExxonMobil’s scale and integration during Middle East disruption, pointing to record Guyana production, increased Permian output, first LNG at Golden Pass, and rapid downstream/logistics response, while outlining specific timing expectations for market normalization after the Strait reopens and acknowledging a multi-year repair window for damaged Qatar LNG trains.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: A look back at the UAE's OPEC membership and who else left the cartel
data.description:
The United Arab Emirates officially left the Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries this week after nearly six decades and quit the broader OPEC+ alliance as well.

Its decision was based on national interest and its commitment to "meeting the market's pressing needs."

The UAE's exit came at a time when countries are grappling with the oil supply disruption caused by the closure of the Strait of Hormuz.

Notably, the UAE has long been OPEC's third-biggest producer, behind Saudi Arabia and Iraq.

The UAE accounted for 12% of OPEC's total production in February, 8% of OPEC+'s total output and about 3% of global oil supply, according to S&P Global Energy.

State-run ADNOC is targeting to raise its production capacity to 5M bpd by 2027, but this effort had been constrained by OPEC's quotas for its members. The most recent quota was a little over 3M bpd.

"Without the constraints of OPEC quotas, the UAE can utilize its spare capacity to pump more oil for a faster and more constructive rebalancing," said Ellen Wald, nonresident senior fellow, Atlantic Council.

"As a 'free agent' with significant spare capacity, ADNOC will be able to pursue new energy strategies and engage foreign investment," she added, mentioning options like building additional pipelines to bypass the Strait of Hormuz.

Other countries that have left OPEC in the recent past are Qatar (in 2019), Indonesia (first in 2008, then again in 2016), Ecuador (in 2020) and Angola (in 2024).

Here's a look at the UAE's journey in the oil cartel:

data.title: BrightSpring forecasts $795M-$825M adjusted EBITDA for 2026 as it closes Community Living divestiture
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: BrightSpring Health Services (BTSG) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "We completed the sale of Community Living to Sevita on March 30, 2026, which resulted in net cash proceeds before tax of approximately $811 million." (Chairman, President & CEO Jon Rousseau) "The proceeds from this transaction will be used to further strengthen the balance sheet, including both debt paydown and cash availability." (Chairman, President & CEO Rousseau)

  • "Overall, we are pleased with our first quarter financial results with total company revenue of $3.6 billion that grew 26% year-over-year." (Chairman, President & CEO Jon Rousseau) "First quarter 2026 adjusted EBITDA of $190 million grew 45% year-over-year with an adjusted EBITDA margin of 5.3%, a 70 basis point improvement year-over-year." (Chairman, President & CEO Rousseau)

  • "In Pharmacy Solutions, the growth outlook is healthy with the specialty and infusion businesses continuing to deliver impressive script growth and patient satisfaction scores." (Chairman, President & CEO Jon Rousseau) "We added 4 exclusive ultra-narrow LDDs to our portfolio in the first quarter, bringing our total number of LDDs to 153." (Chairman, President & CEO Rousseau)

  • "As a reminder, the company's financial results and 2026 guidance pertain to continuing operations and do not include results from the divested Community Living business nor the impact of any future closed acquisitions." (Chairman, President & CEO Jon Rousseau)

  • "Home and Community Pharmacy revenue was $527 million, representing a decline of 9% year-over-year due to an approximately $50 million impact from the IRA, which was expected, along with our decision to exit any uneconomic customers." (CFO, Executive VP & Corporate Secretary Jennifer Phipps)

Outlook

  • "Total revenue is expected to be in the range of $14.725 billion to $15.225 billion, including Pharmacy Solutions revenue of $12.85 billion to $13.3 billion and provider services revenue of $1.875 billion to $1.925 billion." (CFO, Executive VP & Corporate Secretary Jennifer Phipps) "Total adjusted EBITDA is now expected to be in the range of $795 million to $825 million for full year 2026." (CFO Phipps)

  • "This revenue range reflects 14.1% to 17.9% growth over full year 2025, excluding Community Living in both years." (CFO, Executive VP & Corporate Secretary Jennifer Phipps) "This would reflect 28.7% to 33.6% growth over full year 2025, excluding Community Living in both years." (CFO Phipps)

  • "Included in total adjusted EBITDA is expected contribution from the Amedisys and LHC assets acquisition of approximately $30 million." (CFO, Executive VP & Corporate Secretary Jennifer Phipps)

  • "We feel good about the performance of the business through the first 3 months and are on track to deliver the updated full year guidance provided today." (Chairman, President & CEO Jon Rousseau)

Financial Results

  • "In the first quarter of 2026, the total company revenue was $3.6 billion, representing 26% growth from the prior year period." (CFO, Executive VP & Corporate Secretary Jennifer Phipps) "Adjusted EPS for the total company was $0.39 in the first quarter." (CFO Phipps)

  • "Within the Pharmacy Segment, Specialty and Infusion revenue was $2.6 billion, representing growth of 36% from prior year." (CFO, Executive VP & Corporate Secretary Jennifer Phipps) "Home and Community Pharmacy revenue was $527 million, representing a decline of 9% year-over-year." (CFO Phipps)

  • "In the Provider Services segment, we reported revenue of $442 million in the first quarter, which represented 28% growth compared to the prior year." (CFO, Executive VP & Corporate Secretary Jennifer Phipps) "The acquired assets contributed $79 million of revenue and approximately $9 million in adjusted EBITDA in the first quarter." (CFO Phipps)

  • "On a total company basis, cash flow from operations was $123 million in the first quarter." (CFO, Executive VP & Corporate Secretary Jennifer Phipps) "As we look forward to the balance of the year, excluding Community Living related cash flow impact, we expect to deliver approximately $500 million of annual operating cash flow." (CFO Phipps)

  • "Leverage was 2.27x as of March 31, 2026, which declined from 2.99x as of December 31, 2025." (Chairman, President & CEO Jon Rousseau) "As of March 31, net debt outstanding was approximately $1.7 billion." (CFO, Executive VP & Corporate Secretary Jennifer Phipps)

Q&A

  • Ann Hynes, Mizuho Securities: "...expanding the chronic portfolio... what the growth rate is... what drug classes you're focused on?" Chairman, President & CEO Rousseau: "we did see double-digit growth on both the acute side and the chronic specialty side... We did go live in early Q2 with a concierge program around IVIG... building out concierge programs around targeted therapy."

  • Ann Hynes, Mizuho Securities: "...would you be interested in larger M&A... what would be a leverage you would be comfortable going back up to...?" Chairman, President & CEO Rousseau: "under 3x, mid-2s is our longer-term target... we will continue to try to make sure we look at anything that makes the most sense in the long term... we would like to stay within that target leverage range."

  • David Larsen, BTIG: "...general thoughts on the overall Medicare landscape..." Chairman, President & CEO Rousseau: "We've just seen consistency on the Medicare side this year. No major changes... We're going to be applying to this new lead ACO program... Applications are due in May."

  • David Larsen, BTIG: "...update... $600 million of revenue headwind in 2026...?" CFO Phipps: "that's correct... [IRA] about $175 million... full year IRA of about $181 million in Specialty and Infusion and branded generic conversions of about $250 million."

  • Charles Rhyee, TD Cowen: "...still looking at $15 million... headwind on the EBITDA line...?" CFO Phipps: "That is correct."

  • Joanna Gajuk, BofA Securities: "...update... Infusion... are you executing on this already...?" Chairman, President & CEO Rousseau: "we've won 5 LDDs in the last 6 months... We launched IG Connect... we are probably in the early stages of a several year growth focus in LDDs and infusion."

  • Stephen Baxter, Wells Fargo: "...SG&A stepped up like $40 million sequentially... what is driving that...?" CFO Phipps: "there were a number of investments... including into our Salesforce and other areas... commissions and other things that related to the strong sales growth."

  • Erin Wilson Wright, Morgan Stanley: "...PBM dynamic... private label... reimbursement... exposure...?" Chairman, President & CEO Rousseau: "we just don't have a lot of volume or exposure... we're not seeing any big changes or big moves as it relates to our agreements or our products."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst sentiment slightly negative: multiple analysts pressed on PBM/private-label risk and cost cadence, including "the PBMs and the payers are trying to move drugs away" (Brian Tanquilut, Jefferies) and "changes in reimbursement now" (Erin Wilson Wright, Morgan Stanley).

  • Management sentiment slightly positive: management emphasized operational control and steady payer dynamics, including "We're not seeing any big changes" (Chairman, President & CEO Rousseau) and "we have the potential for slight build" in margins (CFO Phipps).

  • Versus last quarter: Q1 included more pointed PBM questions, while management maintained continuity, including "We've just seen consistency... No major changes" (Chairman, President & CEO Rousseau) compared with Q4 framing that "we see a lot of consistency" (Chairman, President & CEO Rousseau).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance ranges moved up versus Q4: Q1 guided "Total revenue... $14.725 billion to $15.225 billion" and "Total adjusted EBITDA... $795 million to $825 million" (CFO Phipps) versus Q4 guidance of "Total revenue... $14.45 billion to $15.0 billion" and "Total adjusted EBITDA... $760 million to $790 million" (CFO Phipps).

  • Balance sheet and divestiture timing advanced: Q4 said Community Living divestiture was expected to "close at the end of the first quarter" with "net after-tax cash proceeds of approximately $715 million" (Chairman, President & CEO Rousseau), while Q1 reported it was completed with "net cash proceeds before tax of approximately $811 million" (Chairman, President & CEO Rousseau).

  • Analyst focus shifted toward PBM/private label and cost run-rate details in Q1, while Q4 leaned more toward home health rates and segment margin direction; management’s responses in both quarters emphasized consistency and operational initiatives.

Risks and Concerns

  • "We expect to see a revenue impact from the IRA of approximately $45 million for each of the remaining quarters of 2026, totaling a Home and Community Pharmacy revenue impact of approximately $175 million for the full year of 2026." (CFO, Executive VP & Corporate Secretary Jennifer Phipps)

  • On PBM/private label concerns, management’s mitigation posture was relationship- and portfolio-based: "we do as best we can to try to partner with everybody" and "we just don't have a lot of concentration internally that would have biosimilar risk" (Chairman, President & CEO Jon Rousseau).

Final Takeaway

BrightSpring positioned Q1 as a start to 2026 that supports higher full-year guidance, highlighted by the completed Community Living sale and lower leverage, while management continued to frame pharmacy growth around LDD expansion, infusion program buildout, and operational/technology initiatives that it said are offsetting IRA-related pressure in Home and Community Pharmacy.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Option Care Health downgraded at BofA on guidance cut
data.description:
Shares of Option Care Health (OPCH) extended a post-earnings decline on Friday after Bank of America downgraded the infusion services provider to Neutral from Buy, citing its lowered full-year revenue outlook.

Reviewing the company’s Q1 print, which included the guidance cut, analyst Joanna Gajuk projected that it will take time for Option Care (OPCH) to offset payor pressures for therapy authorizations and rivalry from pharmacies owned by pharmacy benefit managers.

While the issues are seemingly specific to Stelara, a bowel disease therapy marketed by J&J (JNJ), the analyst reduced her revenue and EBITDA estimates for the company, noting that “we feel more comfortable at the lower end of the guide range given the implied steep ramp-up."

“We expect OPCH to be range-bound until there are more proof points on its ability to return to prior long-term targets,” Gajuk wrote as she nearly halved her price target on the stock to $22 from $40 per share.

data.title: Estée Lauder projects 3%-5% fiscal 2027 sales growth with 12.5%-13% operating margin as it raises fiscal 2026 EPS to $2.35-$2.45
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: The Estée Lauder Companies Inc. (EL) Q3 fiscal 2026

Management View

  • "Today, we raised our fiscal '26 outlook and offered our preliminary view on fiscal '27" and "for the third quarter, organic sales rose 2%" while "operating margin expanded significantly" and "EPS grew 40%" (CEO, President & Director Stephane de la Faverie).

  • "We now expect to deliver organic sales growth of 3%, the high end of our prior range" and "operating margin on track to be 10.7% to 11%" (CEO de la Faverie).

  • "We achieved a significant milestone in the PRGP's program restructuring" and "in April, we expanded the size of the restructuring program" including "the anticipated exit of select unproductive doors in department stores and freestanding store channel" (CEO de la Faverie).

  • "I'm thrilled to welcome to The Estée Lauder Company's portfolio, the #1 prestige skin care brand in India, Forest Essentials" and "in April, we made a minority investment in 111Skin" (CEO de la Faverie).

  • "Overall, we delivered strong performance in the quarter with sales growth, continued margin expansion and strong cash generation" and "diluted EPS was $0.91 for the quarter compared to $0.65 last year" (Executive VP & CFO Akhil Shrivastava).

Outlook

  • "We expect organic net sales growth of approximately 3% at the high end of our prior guidance range" and "diluted EPS is now expected to range between $2.35 and $2.45" for fiscal 2026 (Executive VP & CFO Shrivastava).

  • "For the fourth quarter, we expect an unfavorable impact of approximately 2 percentage points to sales growth and $0.06 to EPS" from Middle East disruptions, while "for the full year, the impact... is expected to be less than 1%" (Executive VP & CFO Shrivastava).

  • "For fiscal '27... we currently assume net sales growth of 3% to 5% for the full year and operating margin of 12.5% to 13.0%" (Executive VP & CFO Shrivastava).

  • Compared with the prior quarter’s outlook language, management moved from a full-year organic sales growth range of "1% to 3%" and operating margin of "9.8% and 10.2%" (Q2 call) to "approximately 3%" organic growth and "10.7% to 11%" operating margin (Q3 call) (Executive VP & CFO Shrivastava).

  • Analysts’ estimates were provided in the prompt, but the fiscal-quarter format is not consistent with the required Q1–Q4 validation rule; as a result, no estimates comparison is included.

Financial Results

  • "Gross margin for the quarter was 76.4%, an expansion of 140 basis points compared to last year" and "operating margin... of 15% compared to 11.4% last year" (Executive VP & CFO Shrivastava).

  • "Organic net sales... grew 2% year-on-year, driven by double-digit growth in fragrance" and "in North America, sales declined low single digits" (Executive VP & CFO Shrivastava).

  • "In terms of restructuring costs, through March 31, we recorded $1.1 billion of total cumulative charges" and "we now expect total restructuring and other charges of $1.5 billion to $1.7 billion before taxes" (Executive VP & CFO Shrivastava).

  • "For the 9 months, we generated $1.2 billion in net cash flows from operating activities" and "we invested $306 million in CapEx" (Executive VP & CFO Shrivastava).

Q&A

  • Dara Mohsenian, Morgan Stanley: Asked about the path back to prior peak margins; CEO de la Faverie said, "Margin recovery is a milestone. It's not a sprint" and framed a path from "8%" at the start of Beauty Reimagined to "12.5% to 13%" in fiscal 2027 preliminary view.

  • Filippo Falorni, Citi: Asked where fiscal 2027 acceleration and share gains come from; CEO de la Faverie said "China is in the fifth quarter of consecutive market share gain" and pointed to diversification across "fragrances in double digit," "online in double digit" and "priority market in double digit" (CEO de la Faverie).

  • Rupesh Parikh, Oppenheimer: Asked whether North America turns to growth in fiscal 2027 and about destocking; CEO de la Faverie answered, "The simple answer is yes" and added "the inventory in North America [is] in a very good position" (CEO de la Faverie).

  • Lauren Lieberman, Barclays: Asked about willingness to exit department stores (including a report on Bobbi Brown); CEO de la Faverie said, "We have to continue to rightsize the department stores" and tied PRGP expansion to "beauty advisers from channels that are dilutive" (CEO de la Faverie); CFO Shrivastava added, "globally, our online business is almost 1/3 of our business" and "even in the U.S., online is getting closer to 40%" (Executive VP & CFO Shrivastava).

  • Christopher Carey, Wells Fargo: Asked about EUKEM and the U.K.; CEO de la Faverie said "EUKEM is a tale of so many different stories" and reported "the U.K. is back in positive territory" while noting, "we are not there yet" (CEO de la Faverie).

  • Bonnie Herzog, Goldman Sachs: Asked how dependent fiscal 2027 margin is on growth vs PRGP, and for a duty-free update; CFO Shrivastava said "sales is a critical part of the driver of margin, but we have multiple tools in the toolkit" (Executive VP & CFO Shrivastava); CEO de la Faverie said travel retail posted "a low single-digit growth" and that Beijing/Shanghai transition impact "has been less than initially expected" (CEO de la Faverie).

Sentiment Analysis

  • Slightly negative analyst tone, centered on durability of margin recovery, North America’s growth inflection, channel exits, and travel retail mechanics (e.g., "Do you think you can get back to the peak high teens margins"; "how critical it is for organic sales growth acceleration") (Dara Mohsenian, Morgan Stanley; Bonnie Herzog, Goldman Sachs).

  • Slightly positive management tone with repeated emphasis on confidence and execution speed, alongside explicit external-risk framing (e.g., "we are confident in the trajectory of our business"; "The current geopolitical and macroeconomic environment remains uncertain") (CEO de la Faverie; Executive VP & CFO Shrivastava).

  • Versus the prior quarter, the current call included more direct boundary-setting and event-risk specificity, including, "The company will not be commenting on the status of discussions with Puig or the possibility of a transaction" and quantified Middle East Q4 headwinds (SVP Finance & Strategy & Head of IR Laraine Mancini; Executive VP & CFO Shrivastava).

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Management raised fiscal 2026 profitability framing from Q2’s operating margin outlook of "9.8% and 10.2%" to Q3’s "10.7% to 11%" and added a preliminary fiscal 2027 target of "12.5% to 13.0%" (Executive VP & CFO Shrivastava).

  • Strategic emphasis broadened from channel expansion updates in Q2 (e.g., Accenture and Shopify selection) to Q3’s added enterprise moves, including "we appointed WPP for a unified enterprise-led approach to media buying" and an "Enterprise Business Services" deployment timeline "by the end of calendar '26" (CEO de la Faverie).

  • Analyst focus shifted from Q2’s travel retail retailer-transition disruption and China promotional cadence to Q3’s longer-dated margin ceiling, U.S. channel exits, and explicit questions on travel retail recovery mechanics (including Beijing/Shanghai duty-free changes) (multiple analysts).

Risks and Concerns

  • "The disruption to our business from the conflict in the Middle East" was cited as a sales headwind in EUKEM, with Q4 assumed impacts of "approximately 2 percentage points" to sales growth and "$0.06" to EPS (Executive VP & CFO Shrivastava).

  • "Retailer bankruptcies, shop-in-shop closures" and "softness for some of our brands" were cited as ongoing North America pressures, alongside planned exits of "select unproductive doors" under the expanded restructuring program (Executive VP & CFO Shrivastava; CEO de la Faverie).

  • "The company will not be commenting on the status of discussions with Puig or the possibility of a transaction" (SVP Finance & Strategy & Head of IR Mancini).

Final Takeaway

Management said it raised fiscal 2026 outlook and introduced a preliminary fiscal 2027 view, anchored by organic net sales growth of approximately 3% in fiscal 2026 with diluted EPS of $2.35 to $2.45, and a fiscal 2027 framework of 3% to 5% net sales growth with 12.5% to 13.0% operating margin. Executives tied margin expansion to PRGP-driven cost actions, channel and door rationalization, and “One ELC” operating ecosystem partners (Accenture, Shopify, WPP), while highlighting market-share and retail momentum in the U.S., Mainland China, and travel retail recovery, amid quantified Middle East disruption assumptions for Q4.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: European automakers hit with increased trade tariffs on EU deal violations
data.description:
European automakers will have to pay an increased 25% tariff for vehicles shipped to the U.S. as a result of accusations that the European Union failed to comply with last year’s trade deal.

"I am pleased to announce that, based on the fact the European Union is not complying with our fully agreed to Trade Deal, next week I will be increasing Tariffs charged to the European Union for Cars and Trucks coming into the United States. The Tariff will be increased to 25%. It is fully understood and agreed that, if they produce Cars and Trucks in U.S.A. Plants, there will be NO TARIFF,” President Trump said in a post on Truth Social Friday.

The post did not specify in what ways the EU broke its trade agreement with the U.S.

Last summer, Trump and European Union President Ursula von der Leyen hammered out the “Turnberry Agreement,” named after Trump’s Scottish golf course. Under the terms of the deal, the U.S. would impose a 15% cap on tariffs for imported goods from the EU, while the EU would eliminate industrial tariffs on U.S. exports and increase access to U.S. agricultural products. The deal also stipulated zero customs duties for both the U.S. and the EU on electronic transmissions.

The agreement—which capped EU auto export tariffs into the U.S. at — was expected to save European automakers as much as $700M a month.

However, after the Supreme Court ruled against the tariffs in February, the European Commission said it expected the U.S. to abide by its commitments to the EU.

“A deal is a deal,” the European Commission said in the wake of the SCOTUS decision. “As the United States’ largest trading partner, the EU expects the U.S. to honor its commitments set out in the Joint Statement—just as the EU stands by its commitments. EU products must continue to benefit from the most competitive treatment, with no increases in tariffs beyond the clear and all-inclusive ceiling previously agreed.”

Related tickers: Mercedes-Benz (MBGAF) (MBGYY), BMW (BMWKY), Volkswagen (VWAGY) (VWAPY), Porsche (DRPRY), Stellantis (STLA), and Ferrari (RACE).

data.title: Mid-to-mega-cap REIT shorts hit extremes in April; XLRE tops S&P 500 YTD
data.description:
The State Street Real Estate Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLRE) closed at 44.39, down -0.02% on the day. The ETF has shown a 10.01% price return YTD, outperforming the S&P 500 (SP500), which has a YTD price return of 5.31%.

XLRE carries a risk grade of D+ with a short interest of 3.48% as a percentage of shares outstanding. 

Short interest in mid-cap to mega-cap ($2B-$200B+) real estate and mortgage REITs shows notable divergence, with some stocks seeing high SI while others have relatively low levels.

Here’s a breakdown of the most and least shorted stocks in the real estate and mortgage REIT sectors:

Top 5 most shorted real estate and mortgage REITs as of April:

  • NETSTREIT (NTST); Short Interest: 29.41%
  • Medical Properties Trust (MPT); Short Interest: 22.75%
  • Park Hotels & Resorts (PK); Short Interest: 17.69%
  • Douglas Emmett (DEI); Short Interest: 16.23%
  • SL Green Realty (SLG); Short Interest: 14.89%

Top 5 least shorted real estate and mortgage REITs as of April:

  • Janus Living (JAN); Short Interest: 0.97%.
  • Prologis (PLD); Short Interest: 1.23%
  • The St. Jo Company (JOE); Short Interest: 1.24%
  • CBRE Group (CBRE); Short Interest: 1.25%
  • American Tower (AMT); Short Interest: 1.47%

This week, mortgage rates have risen following three consecutive weeks of decline. According to the latest Freddie Mac Primary Mortgage Survey, 30-year fixed-rate mortgages averaged 6.30% as of April 30, up from 6.23% last week but still lower than the year-ago level of 6.76%, reported an SA analyst.

This slight rise could impact investor sentiment and market positioning in both sectors. As for the analyst sentiment, XLRE has a sell rating from SA analysts with an average rating of 2.00, while the quantitative rating suggests a hold with a 2.69.

Here are some other notable XLRE REIT ETFs that investors may want to track: iShares Select US REIT ETF (ICF), iShares US Real Estate ETF (IYR), iShares Core US REIT ETF (USRT), Vanguard Real Estate Index Fund ETF (VNQ), iShares Global REIT ETF (REET), iShares Mortgage Real Estate ETF (REM), Vanguard Global ex-US Real Estate Index Fd ETF (VNQI), and iShares Residential and Multisector Real Estate ETF (REZ).

data.title: Trump says he's 'not satisfied' with Iran's latest proposal
data.description:
U.S. President Donald Trump said Friday he is “not satisfied” with a new offer from Iran to end the Middle East war.

“Iran wants to make a deal, but I’m not satisfied with it,” Trump told reporters at the White House. “Iran wants to make a deal because they have no military left.”

Pakistani officials in Islamabad who have been mediating peace talks between the U.S. and Iran reportedly confirmed that Iran sent them an updated proposal to end the conflict. The officials had said they forwarded the proposal to U.S. officials. No details about the contents of Iran’s proposal have yet to be released.

Trump told reporters that Iran has “made strides” in talks with the U.S., “but I’m not sure if they ever get there.”

The president said there “is tremendous discord” among the Iranian leaders, as “they’re having a tremendous problem getting along with each other.”

“In Iran, the leadership is very disjointed. It’s got two to three groups, maybe four, and it’s a very disjointed leadership,” he said. “And with that being said, they all want to make a deal, but they’re all messed up.”

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Spirit Airlines prepares to shut down as rescue deal falters (update)
data.description:
Update 12:55: Adds Trump comments.

Spirit Airlines (FLYYQ) is preparing to cease operations after failing to receive a $500 million lifeline from the US government. Shares of Spirit plunged as much as 74%.

The discount airline wasn't able to get enough support between certain bondholders and the government to get the financing to save the carrier, according to a WSJ report on Friday, which cited people familiar with the matter. 

There was no timetable listed for when operations may cease.President Donald Trump on Friday said the government would offer a final proposal to try to save the airline. 

“If we could do it, we’d do it, but only if it’s a good deal,” Trump told reporters Friday at the White House, saying an announcement could come later Friday or Saturday. “I’d like to save the jobs."

Frontier Airlines (ULCC) and JetBlue (JBLU) could benefit from a competitive standpoint if Spirit (FLYYQ) completely winds down. Shares of Frontier jumped 15% on Friday, while JetBlue rose 12%.

Spirit Airlines began as a Michigan transportation business in 1964 and later became Charter One Airlines in the 1980s. The company launched scheduled passenger service in 1992 and rebranded as Spirit Airlines, building a reputation on low fares and leisure travel routes. After going public in 2011, Spirit (FLYYQ) expanded across the U.S., Caribbean, and Latin America. Over time, Spirit doubled down on its ultra-low-cost model and was well known for charging separately for many extras. More recently, it faced stronger competition and financial strain, leading to the initial bankruptcy filing in 2024.
